policy_statement: Valorise genetic material of agricultural and forestry varieties and species to reduce susceptibility to emerging diseases and pests.
mechanism_description: It valorises genetic material in agricultural and forestry varieties and species to reduce susceptibility to emerging diseases and pests, improving climate-related resilience of crops and forests.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate change–amplified pests/diseases → ↓ susceptibility of crops/forests (via genetic diversity/resistant varieties) → ↓ impact on yields/biomass and livelihoods
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Land managers, breeders, and forestry/agricultural systems select, conserve, and deploy genetic resources (e.g., resistant cultivars, diverse provenance planting material, seed banks) rather than relying on narrow, susceptible genotypes.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Increased genetic diversity and targeted resistance traits reduce pathogen/insect establishment, spread, and damage severity, improving stand/crop health under shifting temperature/precipitation regimes.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower vulnerability to climate-related pest/disease outbreaks, reducing yield/biomass losses and stabilizing ecosystem services and rural incomes (adaptation/resilience benefit).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced crop/forest losses and productivity shocks under climate-driven pest and disease pressure (i.e., increased resilience to climate impacts).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism primarily reduces damage from climate-intensified pests and diseases by improving biological resilience of managed ecosystems.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be secondary mitigation co-benefits (e.g., avoiding deforestation or replanting emissions if forests survive), the direct and intended effect is impact reduction under climate hazards—an adaptation pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy frames genetic valorisation mainly to increase carbon sequestration (e.g., selecting high-biomass genotypes) it could shift toward mitigation; however, this canonical mechanism is explicitly about pest/disease resilience, so adaptation remains dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that valorises/deploys genetic resources to reduce susceptibility to emerging pests/diseases is fundamentally aimed at reducing climate-impact risk to crops/forests, fitting Adaptation consistently.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any finer assignment (e.g., “agriculture resilience,” “forest health,” “biosecurity”) depends on policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential co-benefits include reduced pesticide use (resource efficiency/mitigation) and avoided land-use change emissions if yields/forest survival improve; classification remains Adaptation because the dominant stated pathway is resilience to climate-amplified pests and diseases.
key_indicators: “Valorisation of the genetic material”; “agricultural and forestry varieties and species”; “reduce susceptibility”; “emerging diseases and pests”
co_benefits: Potential biodiversity conservation (genetic diversity), reduced pesticide use, yield stability/rural income support, improved ecosystem resilience (may also support wildfire resilience indirectly via healthier stands)
instance_edge_case_notes: “Valorisation” is ambiguous and could include non-financial measures (e.g., conservation strategy, certification). In absence of explicit mandates/standards/targets, it is classified as an enabling incentive/programmatic measure rather than regulation or performance standard.
